---
title: "Span and Subspaces"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Geometry of Data"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from mpl_toolkits.mplot3d import Axes3D  # noqa: F401

# One restrained visual style for every figure in this article, matched to the
# rest of the Geometry-of-Data track.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

VEC    = "#2b3a67"   # the spanning vectors themselves
SPAN   = "#c1121f"   # the span (reachable set)
ACCENT = "#0a7d4d"   # highlights / target points
GREY   = "#6c757d"
PALE   = "#9fb3d1"


def draw_vec(ax, tail, head, color=VEC, lw=2.4, label=None, z=5):
    """Draw a 2-D arrow from tail to head with a clean head."""
    ax.annotate("", xy=head, xytext=tail,
                arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color=color, lw=lw,
                                shrinkA=0, shrinkB=0), zorder=z)
    if label is not None:
        mid = 0.5 * (np.asarray(tail) + np.asarray(head))
        ax.text(head[0], head[1], label, color=color, fontsize=12,
                ha="left", va="bottom")
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This is the third article in the **Geometry of
> Data** track and the first to ask a question about *whole sets* of vectors rather
> than one at a time. It assumes [GEOM-00 *What Is a Vector, Really?*] — that a vector
> is simultaneously an arrow, a list of numbers in $\mathbb{R}^n$, and one row of a
> dataset — and [GEOM-01 *Adding and Scaling: The Linear Combination*], which gave us
> the only two operations linear algebra permits and packaged them into the **linear
> combination** $c_1\mathbf{v}_1 + \cdots + c_k\mathbf{v}_k$. Here we ask what happens
> when we turn those coefficients loose and let them range over *every* real number.
> The reachable set we get is the **span**, and the shape it always forms is a
> **subspace**. This sets up [GEOM-03 *Linear Independence, Basis, and Dimension*]
> (which spanning sets carry no redundancy), [GEOM-06 *Orthogonal Projection*] (we
> always project onto a subspace), and [GEOM-12 *The Four Fundamental Subspaces*] (the
> column space and null space are spans and subspaces by construction).

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

Hand someone a few vectors and the two operations from [GEOM-01] — add, and scale by any
real number — and ask: *exactly which points can they now reach?* The answer is the
**span**, the set of all linear combinations of the given vectors. This article shows
that the span is never an arbitrary blob: it is always a flat sheet through the origin —
a point, a line, a plane, or a higher-dimensional analogue — a structure we name a
**subspace**. We define a subspace by two closure properties, prove the span always
satisfies them, and prove that *every* subspace is the span of some set of vectors, so
the two ideas are two faces of one object. The single claim the article defends is that
**the dimension of the span is decided by the vectors' geometric relationships, not by
how many of them you were handed** — three vectors can span a plane, two can span a line,
and recognizing when "more vectors" buys you "no more reach" is the linear-algebra core
of multicollinearity, the failure mode that haunts regression.

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
The **span** of a set of vectors is *everywhere you can get to* by adding and scaling
them — the set of all their linear combinations. That reachable set is never ragged: it
is always a **subspace**, a flat region that must pass through the origin and is sealed
shut under addition and scaling (combine two of its points, or stretch one, and you never
leave it). How *big* the span is — a line, a plane, a 3-D solid — is set by how the
vectors point relative to one another, **not** by how many you have. Extra vectors that
already lie inside what you can reach add nothing.
:::

Everything below orbits that box. Two ideas live in it, and they turn out to be the same
idea seen from two directions. **Span** is constructive — *start from vectors, build the
reachable set.* **Subspace** is descriptive — *here is a set; is it closed and flat?* We
will prove that every span is a subspace (@thm-span-is-subspace) and that every subspace
is a span (@thm-subspace-is-span), which is why the track uses the words almost
interchangeably from here on. Hold onto the last sentence of the box especially: it is
the seed of @sec-collinear, where two vectors that *look* like they should fill a plane
collapse to a line — the geometry behind a regression that refuses to give a stable
answer.

# Why we are here: the problem of "what can I build?" {#sec-why}

Keep one question in view for the whole article: **given the raw materials, what is the
complete set of things I can build, and what shape does that set have?** Every definition
and proof below is an answer to some piece of that question.

## The question is older than linear algebra

The span question predates the vocabulary we use for it by a long way. When a 19th-century
surveyor fixed a location by combining measured displacements — *so far east, so far
north* — he was implicitly asking which points on the map were reachable from a base
station using his available directions of travel. When a chemist asked whether a target
compound could be assembled from a given set of reactant species in fixed proportions, the
arithmetic of "how many of each" is exactly the arithmetic of linear combinations, and the
question "can I make this product at all?" is the question "is this target *in the span* of
my reactants?" The recurring pattern is always the same: a fixed set of building blocks, a
rule for combining them (scale each, then add), and a question about **reach**.

Hermann Grassmann, in his 1844 *Ausdehnungslehre* ("Theory of Extension"), was the first
to abstract this pattern away from any particular physical setting. He studied collections
of "extensive quantities" closed under combination and recognized that the *structure* —
not the surveyor's displacements or the chemist's reactants — was the real object of
study. Giuseppe Peano sharpened it into the modern axioms in 1888, and by the early
twentieth century "the set of all linear combinations" and "a set closed under combination"
had crystallized into **span** and **subspace**. We inherit Grassmann's insight directly:
the moment you have building blocks and a combination rule, the set of reachable things has
a rigid, predictable shape, and naming that shape is most of the battle.

## Why this matters for data, right now

In [GEOM-00] we committed to reading a data table geometrically: each of the $p$ feature
columns is a direction in space, and each of the $n$ rows is a point. The span question is
then immediate and unavoidable. The columns of a data matrix are vectors; **the set of all
their linear combinations is the set of every outcome a linear model could ever predict.**
When we later fit a regression, the fitted values $\hat{\mathbf{y}}$ are forced to live in
the span of the predictor columns — that is *all* a linear model can produce. Whether a
target vector $\mathbf{y}$ can be hit exactly, or only approached, is precisely the
question "is $\mathbf{y}$ in the span of the columns?" And the dimension of that span — how
much genuinely independent information the columns carry — is the difference between a
model that estimates cleanly and one that cannot tell two of its own coefficients apart.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"In one sentence, why should a working data scientist care about span and subspaces?"*
Because the span of your feature columns is the entire universe of predictions a linear
model can make, and the *dimension* of that span is how much non-redundant information your
features actually carry. When two features are (nearly) linear combinations of others, the
span does not grow, the model has fewer real degrees of freedom than columns, and
coefficients become unstable — that is **multicollinearity** ([REGR-07]), and it *is* the
span collapsing. Everything geometric about why a design matrix is "rank-deficient" is in
this article.
:::

# Intuition before symbols {#sec-intuition}

Before any formal definitions, build the picture with your hands. We are in the plane
$\mathbb{R}^2$ or in space $\mathbb{R}^3$, and we have a small handful of arrows planted at
the origin. The rule from [GEOM-01] lets us do exactly two things to them: **stretch or flip
any arrow by a real factor** (scalar multiplication) and **lay arrows tip-to-tail and read
off the resulting arrow** (addition). The span is the answer to "sweep every dial": let
every scaling factor run over all real numbers, add the results in every combination, and
mark every point you can land on.

Start with **one nonzero vector** $\mathbf{v}$. The only moves are $c\mathbf{v}$ for a real
number $c$. As $c$ runs from $-\infty$ to $+\infty$, the tip of $c\mathbf{v}$ traces out
the entire **line through the origin** in the direction of $\mathbf{v}$ — both ways, and
through the origin itself (at $c=0$). One vector spans a line.

Now take **two vectors** $\mathbf{v}_1, \mathbf{v}_2$. A combination $c_1\mathbf{v}_1 +
c_2\mathbf{v}_2$ has two independent dials. If the two arrows point in genuinely different
directions, sweeping both dials fills an entire **plane through the origin**. But — and
this is the whole drama of the article — if $\mathbf{v}_2$ happens to point along the *same
line* as $\mathbf{v}_1$ (say $\mathbf{v}_2 = 2\mathbf{v}_1$), then every combination
$c_1\mathbf{v}_1 + c_2\mathbf{v}_2 = (c_1 + 2c_2)\mathbf{v}_1$ is *still just a multiple of
$\mathbf{v}_1$*. The second dial buys you nothing. Two vectors, but only a line. @fig-spans
shows both outcomes side by side.

```{python}
#| label: fig-spans
#| fig-cap: "What a span looks like in the plane. **Left:** one vector $\\mathbf{v}$ spans the line of all its multiples $c\\mathbf{v}$ (red), passing through the origin both ways. **Center:** two vectors pointing in different directions span the *entire plane* — every point is reachable, shown by the shaded fill and a sample combination $1.5\\mathbf{v}_1 - 0.5\\mathbf{v}_2$ (green). **Right:** two vectors on the same line ($\\mathbf{v}_2 = 1.8\\,\\mathbf{v}_1$) span only that line — the second vector adds no reach. The number of vectors does not decide the size of the span; their directions do."
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(9.4, 3.5))

# --- Left: one vector spans a line ---
ax = axes[0]
v = np.array([1.4, 0.9])
ts = np.linspace(-2.4, 2.4, 50)
line = np.outer(ts, v)
ax.plot(line[:, 0], line[:, 1], color=SPAN, lw=2.2, label=r"span$\{\mathbf{v}\}$")
draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), v, color=VEC, label=r"$\mathbf{v}$")
ax.set_title("one vector: a line")
ax.set_xlim(-3.5, 3.5); ax.set_ylim(-3, 3)

# --- Center: two independent vectors span the plane ---
ax = axes[1]
v1 = np.array([1.6, 0.4])
v2 = np.array([0.3, 1.5])
ax.fill([-4, 4, 4, -4], [-4, -4, 4, 4], color=SPAN, alpha=0.10)
combo = 1.5 * v1 - 0.5 * v2
draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), v1, color=VEC, label=r"$\mathbf{v}_1$")
draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), v2, color=VEC, label=r"$\mathbf{v}_2$")
draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), combo, color=ACCENT)
ax.text(combo[0], combo[1], r"$1.5\mathbf{v}_1-0.5\mathbf{v}_2$",
        color=ACCENT, fontsize=9, ha="right", va="top")
ax.set_title("two directions: the plane")
ax.set_xlim(-3.5, 3.5); ax.set_ylim(-3, 3)

# --- Right: two collinear vectors span only a line ---
ax = axes[2]
v1 = np.array([1.4, 0.9])
v2 = 1.8 * v1
ax.plot(line[:, 0], line[:, 1], color=SPAN, lw=2.2)
draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), v2, color=PALE, label=r"$\mathbf{v}_2=1.8\mathbf{v}_1$")
draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), v1, color=VEC, label=r"$\mathbf{v}_1$")
ax.set_title("two collinear: still a line")
ax.set_xlim(-3.5, 3.5); ax.set_ylim(-3, 3)

for ax in axes:
    ax.axhline(0, color=GREY, lw=0.8); ax.axvline(0, color=GREY, lw=0.8)
    ax.set_aspect("equal"); ax.legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=8, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

Three observations from the picture will become the formal content of the article, so name
them now:

1. **Every span contains the origin.** Set all the dials to zero and you are back at
   $\mathbf{0}$. There is no way to build a span that floats off the origin.
2. **A span is flat and has no edges.** It is a line, a plane, a 3-D solid through the
   origin — never a curve, never a half-plane, never a region with a boundary. You can
   always step further along it or combine two of its points and stay inside.
3. **The size of a span is bounded by the number of vectors but decided by their
   directions.** $k$ vectors can span *at most* a $k$-dimensional region, and exactly $k$
   only when none of them is reachable from the others. Redundant directions cost you
   dimension.

The rest of the article makes each of these precise and proves it. We start with span
(the construction), define subspace (the shape), prove they coincide, and then spend the
back half on the honest edge case in observation 3 — the collapse that data scientists
meet as multicollinearity.

# The formal development {#sec-formal}

## Linear combinations, recalled precisely {#sec-recall}

We lean on one object from [GEOM-01], restated here so nothing is implicit. Throughout,
**vectors are columns in $\mathbb{R}^n$** (an ordered list of $n$ real numbers, written
vertically), boldface denotes a vector, and lowercase italics denote real-number scalars.

::: {#def-lincomb}
**Definition (linear combination).** Given vectors $\mathbf{v}_1,\dots,\mathbf{v}_k \in
\mathbb{R}^n$ and real scalars $c_1,\dots,c_k \in \mathbb{R}$, the vector

$$
c_1\mathbf{v}_1 + c_2\mathbf{v}_2 + \cdots + c_k\mathbf{v}_k
\;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{k} c_j \mathbf{v}_j
$$ {#eq-lincomb}

is a **linear combination** of the $\mathbf{v}_j$ with **coefficients** (or **weights**)
$c_j$. The sum and the scalar multiples are taken **componentwise**: the $i$-th entry of
the result is $\sum_{j=1}^k c_j (\mathbf{v}_j)_i$, where $(\mathbf{v}_j)_i$ is the $i$-th
entry of $\mathbf{v}_j$.
:::

Two facts about @eq-lincomb, both established in [GEOM-01], are the only algebra we need and
we will invoke them by name:

- **Closure of the combination form.** Adding two linear combinations of the same vectors,
  or scaling one, produces another linear combination of those same vectors — because
  componentwise addition and scalar multiplication of real numbers stay real. We re-derive
  exactly this when we prove @thm-span-is-subspace, so it is not assumed silently.
- **The zero combination.** Taking every $c_j = 0$ gives $\sum_j 0\cdot\mathbf{v}_j =
  \mathbf{0}$, the zero vector (all entries zero). This is the single fact behind "every
  span contains the origin."

## Span: the set of everything reachable {#sec-span-def}

::: {#def-span}
**Definition (span).** The **span** of vectors $\mathbf{v}_1,\dots,\mathbf{v}_k \in
\mathbb{R}^n$ is the set of *all* their linear combinations:

$$
\operatorname{span}\{\mathbf{v}_1,\dots,\mathbf{v}_k\}
\;=\;
\Bigl\{\, \sum_{j=1}^{k} c_j \mathbf{v}_j \;:\; c_1,\dots,c_k \in \mathbb{R} \,\Bigr\}.
$$ {#eq-span}

A vector $\mathbf{w}$ **is in the span** (we write $\mathbf{w} \in
\operatorname{span}\{\mathbf{v}_1,\dots,\mathbf{v}_k\}$) if and only if there exist real
coefficients $c_1,\dots,c_k$ with $\mathbf{w} = \sum_j c_j \mathbf{v}_j$.
:::

Read the set-builder notation in @eq-span slowly, because it carries the entire idea. The
expression to the left of the colon, $\sum_j c_j \mathbf{v}_j$, is the *shape* of a typical
element. The condition to the right of the colon, "$c_1,\dots,c_k \in \mathbb{R}$," says
the coefficients range over **all** real numbers, independently. The braces collect every
vector you obtain as the dials sweep their full range. So the span is not one combination —
it is the entire catalogue of them.

A second, equally important reading: **"$\mathbf{w}$ is in the span" is exactly the
question "does a certain system of linear equations have a solution?"** Writing each vector
out by components, $\mathbf{w} = \sum_j c_j\mathbf{v}_j$ is $n$ scalar equations (one per
entry) in the $k$ unknowns $c_1,\dots,c_k$. The vector $\mathbf{w}$ is reachable precisely
when those equations are *consistent* — when some assignment of weights solves them. This
is the bridge from the geometric word *span* to the computational world of solving
$A\mathbf{c} = \mathbf{w}$, which the track makes fully explicit in [GEOM-08] (a matrix
times a vector *is* a linear combination of the matrix's columns) and [GEOM-11] (when that
system has a solution). We will use this reading in @sec-membership to *test* membership by
hand.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Is the span a finite set, an infinite set, or what?"* For any nonzero vector it is
**infinite** — uncountably so — because the coefficients range over all of $\mathbb{R}$,
a continuum. The only finite span is $\operatorname{span}\{\mathbf{0}\} = \{\mathbf{0}\}$,
a single point. Do not picture the span as "the $k$ vectors you were handed plus a few
combinations"; picture it as the *entire continuous sheet* they sweep out.
:::

## Subspace: the shape a span always has {#sec-subspace-def}

We now define the kind of *shape* a span turns out to be, independently of any particular
spanning vectors. The definition isolates the two structural properties we read off the
pictures in @sec-intuition: a subspace is a set you cannot escape by combining or scaling
its own members.

::: {#def-subspace}
**Definition (subspace of $\mathbb{R}^n$).** A set $V \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$ is a
**subspace** if it satisfies all three conditions:

1. **(Contains the origin.)** $\mathbf{0} \in V$.
2. **(Closed under addition.)** If $\mathbf{u} \in V$ and $\mathbf{w} \in V$, then
   $\mathbf{u} + \mathbf{w} \in V$.
3. **(Closed under scalar multiplication.)** If $\mathbf{u} \in V$ and $c \in \mathbb{R}$,
   then $c\,\mathbf{u} \in V$.
:::

The word *closed* means exactly what it says in plain English: the operation cannot take
you out of the set. Add two members — still inside. Scale a member — still inside. The
geometric translation of these three rules is the heart of the matter and worth stating in
words: **a subspace is a flat object (no curving, since combinations of its points stay
inside) that has no boundary (you can always scale further) and is pinned to the origin.**
Lines and planes *through the origin* qualify; a line that misses the origin does not, and
neither does any curve, any line segment, or any region with an edge.

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**Condition 1 is not optional decoration, and a set can fail it loudly.** Consider the line
$y = x + 1$ in $\mathbb{R}^2$ — points of the form $(t,\,t+1)$. It is perfectly flat and
straight, so it *looks* like it should be a subspace. It is not: it does not pass through
the origin ($\mathbf{0} = (0,0)$ would need $0 = 0 + 1$, false). And it fails closure too —
add $(0,1)$ and $(1,2)$, both on the line, and you get $(1,3)$, which is *not* on the line
($3 \neq 1+1$). A flat set that misses the origin is an **affine** set, not a subspace; the
two are constantly confused. The fix, when you meet such a set in practice (e.g. a
regression line, or data that has not been centered), is to *translate it to the origin* —
exactly the "subtract the mean" centering move that the data tracks use before applying any
of this machinery. After centering, the affine set becomes a genuine subspace.
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why are conditions 2 and 3 stated separately — isn't closure under scaling implied by
closure under addition?"* No. Addition lets you reach $\mathbf{u}+\mathbf{u} = 2\mathbf{u}$,
$3\mathbf{u}$, and so on — but only **integer** (in fact only positive-integer, then with
condition 1 the zero) multiples, by repeated addition. It does **not** give you
$\tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{u}$ or $-\sqrt{2}\,\mathbf{u}$. Scalar closure (condition 3) is what
guarantees the *whole continuous line* through each point, including fractional and
negative and irrational multiples. The two conditions together are equivalent to the
single statement "closed under linear combinations," which is the form we prove next. And
condition 1 is not redundant either in the strictest reading: the **empty set** is
vacuously closed under both operations but is not a subspace — requiring $\mathbf{0}\in V$
rules it out. (When $V$ is nonempty, condition 1 does follow from condition 3 by taking
$c=0$; we list it anyway to make "contains the origin" impossible to forget.)
:::

## Span is a subspace {#sec-span-is-subspace}

The first half of "span and subspace are the same object" is to show every span satisfies
@def-subspace. We prove it in full; every closure check is on the page.

::: {#thm-span-is-subspace}
**Theorem (every span is a subspace).** For any vectors
$\mathbf{v}_1,\dots,\mathbf{v}_k \in \mathbb{R}^n$, the set
$V = \operatorname{span}\{\mathbf{v}_1,\dots,\mathbf{v}_k\}$ is a subspace of
$\mathbb{R}^n$.
:::

::: {.proof}
We verify the three conditions of @def-subspace one at a time.

**Condition 1 — the origin is in $V$.** Choose every coefficient to be zero. By @eq-span
with $c_1 = \cdots = c_k = 0$,

$$
\sum_{j=1}^k 0\cdot\mathbf{v}_j = \mathbf{0},
$$

so $\mathbf{0}$ is a linear combination of the $\mathbf{v}_j$ and therefore $\mathbf{0}
\in V$. (This is the "zero combination" fact from @sec-recall, used explicitly.)

**Condition 2 — closed under addition.** Take any two members $\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{w} \in
V$. By the definition of the span, *being in $V$ means being writable as a combination*, so
there exist real coefficients $a_1,\dots,a_k$ and $b_1,\dots,b_k$ with

$$
\mathbf{u} = \sum_{j=1}^k a_j \mathbf{v}_j,
\qquad
\mathbf{w} = \sum_{j=1}^k b_j \mathbf{v}_j .
$$

Add them, and group the two sums term by term — legal because vector addition is done
componentwise and real addition is associative and commutative (the vector-space rules from
[GEOM-01]):

$$
\mathbf{u} + \mathbf{w}
= \sum_{j=1}^k a_j \mathbf{v}_j + \sum_{j=1}^k b_j \mathbf{v}_j
= \sum_{j=1}^k \bigl(a_j \mathbf{v}_j + b_j \mathbf{v}_j\bigr)
= \sum_{j=1}^k (a_j + b_j)\,\mathbf{v}_j .
$$

The last equality factors $\mathbf{v}_j$ out of each pair using the distributive rule
$a_j\mathbf{v}_j + b_j\mathbf{v}_j = (a_j+b_j)\mathbf{v}_j$ (again from [GEOM-01]). Now define
$c_j = a_j + b_j$. Each $c_j$ is a sum of two real numbers, hence a real number. So

$$
\mathbf{u} + \mathbf{w} = \sum_{j=1}^k c_j \mathbf{v}_j
$$

is a linear combination of the $\mathbf{v}_j$ with real coefficients — which is precisely
the definition of belonging to $V$. Therefore $\mathbf{u} + \mathbf{w} \in V$.

**Condition 3 — closed under scalar multiplication.** Take any member $\mathbf{u} \in V$,
say $\mathbf{u} = \sum_{j=1}^k a_j \mathbf{v}_j$, and any real number $c$. Scale:

$$
c\,\mathbf{u}
= c\sum_{j=1}^k a_j \mathbf{v}_j
= \sum_{j=1}^k c\,(a_j \mathbf{v}_j)
= \sum_{j=1}^k (c\,a_j)\,\mathbf{v}_j .
$$

The first equality pulls the scalar $c$ inside the sum (scalar multiplication distributes
over vector addition); the second regroups $c(a_j\mathbf{v}_j) = (ca_j)\mathbf{v}_j$ by the
associativity of scalar multiplication — both [GEOM-01] rules. Each new coefficient $ca_j$
is a product of two reals, hence real. So $c\mathbf{u}$ is again a linear combination of the
$\mathbf{v}_j$, meaning $c\mathbf{u} \in V$.

All three conditions hold, so $V$ is a subspace. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

That proof is short, but notice what made it work: closure of the *span* is nothing more
than closure of the real numbers under $+$ and $\times$, lifted componentwise through the
linear-combination form. This is the precise sense in which "you can never escape a span by
combining its members" — the escape attempt always lands you on another valid choice of
coefficients.

## Every subspace is a span {#sec-subspace-is-span}

The other half closes the loop. We have shown every span *is* a subspace; now we show every
subspace can be *written as* a span. Together they say the two words describe one class of
objects.

::: {#thm-subspace-is-span}
**Theorem (every subspace is a span).** Let $V \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$ be a subspace. Then
there exist finitely many vectors $\mathbf{v}_1,\dots,\mathbf{v}_k \in V$ such that
$V = \operatorname{span}\{\mathbf{v}_1,\dots,\mathbf{v}_k\}$.
:::

::: {.proof}
We build a spanning set by hand, one vector at a time, and show the construction stops.

If $V = \{\mathbf{0}\}$, then $V = \operatorname{span}\{\mathbf{0}\}$ and we are done with
$k=1$ (indeed the zero set is the span of the single zero vector). So assume $V$ contains a
nonzero vector.

**The greedy construction.** Pick any nonzero $\mathbf{v}_1 \in V$. Because $V$ is closed
under linear combinations (it is a subspace), *every* combination of vectors drawn from $V$
stays in $V$; in particular $\operatorname{span}\{\mathbf{v}_1\} \subseteq V$. Now ask: is
$\operatorname{span}\{\mathbf{v}_1\}$ already all of $V$? If yes, stop. If no, there is some
$\mathbf{v}_2 \in V$ that is *not* in $\operatorname{span}\{\mathbf{v}_1\}$; adjoin it.
Again $\operatorname{span}\{\mathbf{v}_1,\mathbf{v}_2\} \subseteq V$ (same closure
argument). Repeat: at each step, if the span of the chosen vectors is not yet all of $V$,
there is a vector in $V$ outside that span, and we adjoin it.

**Why it stops.** Each adjoined vector lies *outside* the span of the previous ones, so it
is not a linear combination of them — it is a genuinely new direction. We will prove in
[GEOM-03] that in $\mathbb{R}^n$ you cannot have more than $n$ vectors that are each outside
the span of the rest (any $n+1$ vectors in $\mathbb{R}^n$ are linearly dependent — at least
one is a combination of the others). We flag that fact here as imported from the next
article rather than assumed silently: it is the **dimension bound**, and its full proof is
[GEOM-03]'s job. Granting it, the greedy process cannot adjoin more than $n$ vectors, so it
must halt after some $k \le n$ steps with

$$
\operatorname{span}\{\mathbf{v}_1,\dots,\mathbf{v}_k\} = V .
$$

The two inclusions that close the argument: $\operatorname{span}\{\mathbf{v}_1,\dots,
\mathbf{v}_k\} \subseteq V$ because every $\mathbf{v}_j \in V$ and $V$ is closed under
combinations; and $V \subseteq \operatorname{span}\{\mathbf{v}_1,\dots,\mathbf{v}_k\}$
because the process only stopped once the span had swallowed all of $V$. Equal sets.
$\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

::: {.callout-note title="What we borrowed, and where it is built"}
This proof leaned on one fact from the next article: *in $\mathbb{R}^n$ you cannot keep
finding vectors each outside the span of the previous ones beyond $n$ of them.* That is the
**dimension bound**, proved in full in [GEOM-03 *Linear Independence, Basis, and
Dimension*]. We imported only its statement so the loop "span $\Leftrightarrow$ subspace"
could close here. Nothing else in the present article depends on it — every other result is
self-contained.
:::

With both theorems in hand, the vocabulary settles: from now on, "the subspace spanned by
the columns of $A$" and "the column space of $A$" and "$\operatorname{span}$ of the
columns" all name the same object, and we will use whichever phrasing the geometry makes
clearest.

## Testing membership: is a target vector reachable? {#sec-membership}

The constructive payoff of @def-span is a *procedure*: to decide whether a target
$\mathbf{w}$ is in $\operatorname{span}\{\mathbf{v}_1,\dots,\mathbf{v}_k\}$, ask whether the
system $\sum_j c_j\mathbf{v}_j = \mathbf{w}$ has a solution in the unknown coefficients
$c_j$. Let us run it once, fully by hand, on a small concrete case so the mechanics are
unmysterious.

Take in $\mathbb{R}^3$ the two vectors

$$
\mathbf{v}_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix},
\qquad
\mathbf{v}_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix},
$$

and ask two membership questions.

**Question A: is $\mathbf{w} = (2,\,3,\,5)^\top$ in the span?** We need reals $c_1, c_2$
with $c_1\mathbf{v}_1 + c_2\mathbf{v}_2 = \mathbf{w}$. Writing the three components
separately (each entry of the combination must match the corresponding entry of
$\mathbf{w}$):

$$
\underbrace{c_1 \cdot 1 + c_2 \cdot 0}_{\text{entry 1}} = 2,
\qquad
\underbrace{c_1 \cdot 0 + c_2 \cdot 1}_{\text{entry 2}} = 3,
\qquad
\underbrace{c_1 \cdot 1 + c_2 \cdot 1}_{\text{entry 3}} = 5 .
$$

The first equation gives $c_1 = 2$ outright; the second gives $c_2 = 3$ outright. Those are
forced. Now the third equation is not a new unknown to solve — it is a **consistency
check**: substituting, $c_1 + c_2 = 2 + 3 = 5$, which matches the required $5$ exactly. The
system is consistent, so $\mathbf{w}$ **is** in the span, with the explicit recipe
$\mathbf{w} = 2\mathbf{v}_1 + 3\mathbf{v}_2$.

**Question B: is $\mathbf{w}' = (2,\,3,\,0)^\top$ in the span?** Same first two equations
force $c_1 = 2$, $c_2 = 3$. But the third equation now demands $c_1 + c_2 = 0$, i.e.
$2 + 3 = 0$, which is $5 = 0$ — **false**. There is no choice of coefficients that satisfies
all three equations at once; the system is *inconsistent*. So $\mathbf{w}'$ is **not** in
the span. Geometrically, $\mathbf{w}'$ sticks out of the plane that $\mathbf{v}_1$ and
$\mathbf{v}_2$ sweep.

The pattern generalizes and is worth stating: **the span of these two vectors is exactly
the set of $(a,b,c)^\top$ with $c = a + b$** — a single linear constraint carves the plane
out of all of $\mathbb{R}^3$. The third coordinate is not free; it is dictated by the first
two. That one equation *is* the plane, and it is why two vectors span a (two-dimensional)
plane inside (three-dimensional) space rather than filling it. @fig-plane-in-r3 draws this
plane, the reachable target, and the unreachable one.

```{python}
#| label: fig-plane-in-r3
#| fig-cap: "The span of $\\mathbf{v}_1=(1,0,1)$ and $\\mathbf{v}_2=(0,1,1)$ is the plane $\\{(a,b,c): c=a+b\\}$ through the origin in $\\mathbb{R}^3$ (shaded). The reachable target $\\mathbf{w}=(2,3,5)$ (green) lies *on* the plane — it satisfies $5=2+3$ — and equals $2\\mathbf{v}_1+3\\mathbf{v}_2$. The target $\\mathbf{w}'=(2,3,0)$ (black) floats *off* the plane ($0\\neq 2+3$) and is unreachable. A span in $\\mathbb{R}^3$ is a flat sheet through the origin, never a curved or floating surface."
v1 = np.array([1, 0, 1.0])
v2 = np.array([0, 1, 1.0])
w_in  = np.array([2, 3, 5.0])    # = 2 v1 + 3 v2, on the plane
w_out = np.array([2, 3, 0.0])    # off the plane

# Mesh the plane c = a + b over a window.
a = np.linspace(-1, 4, 12)
b = np.linspace(-1, 4, 12)
A, B = np.meshgrid(a, b)
C = A + B

fig = plt.figure(figsize=(6.6, 5.2))
ax = fig.add_subplot(111, projection="3d")
ax.plot_surface(A, B, C, color=SPAN, alpha=0.22, linewidth=0)

for v, name, col in [(v1, r"$\mathbf{v}_1$", VEC), (v2, r"$\mathbf{v}_2$", VEC)]:
    ax.quiver(0, 0, 0, *v, color=col, lw=2.4, arrow_length_ratio=0.12)
    ax.text(*v, name, color=col, fontsize=12)

ax.quiver(0, 0, 0, *w_in, color=ACCENT, lw=2.4, arrow_length_ratio=0.08)
ax.text(*w_in, r"  $\mathbf{w}=(2,3,5)$ (in span)", color=ACCENT, fontsize=10)
ax.scatter(*w_out, color="black", s=55)
# dashed gap from w_out down to the plane (the plane height at (2,3) is 5)
ax.plot([w_out[0], w_out[0]], [w_out[1], w_out[1]], [w_out[2], 5],
        color="black", ls="--", lw=1.4)
ax.text(*w_out, r"  $\mathbf{w}'=(2,3,0)$ (off span)", color="black", fontsize=10)

ax.set_xlabel("$a$"); ax.set_ylabel("$b$"); ax.set_zlabel("$c$")
ax.set_title(r"span$\{\mathbf{v}_1,\mathbf{v}_2\}=\{c=a+b\}$")
ax.view_init(elev=20, azim=-60)
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Given some vectors, how do you actually check by hand whether a target is in their span?"*
Set up the system $\sum_j c_j\mathbf{v}_j = \mathbf{w}$ — one equation per coordinate — and
ask if it is consistent. Concretely you stack the spanning vectors as the columns of a
matrix $A$ and row-reduce the augmented matrix $[\,A \mid \mathbf{w}\,]$: if you ever reach
a row of the form $[\,0\ \cdots\ 0 \mid (\text{nonzero})\,]$, the system is inconsistent and
$\mathbf{w}$ is **not** in the span; otherwise it is, and the reduction hands you the
coefficients. This is exactly the $A\mathbf{c}=\mathbf{w}$ solvability question that
[GEOM-11 *Solving $Ax=b$*] treats in full — span-membership and system-consistency are the
same question wearing different clothes.
:::

## How big is the span? The dimension count, previewed honestly {#sec-howbig}

We have repeatedly said "the number of vectors does not decide the size of the span." We
can now make that precise enough to use, while being honest that the *complete* theory of
dimension is the next article's.

From the membership view, the span of $k$ vectors is the set of right-hand sides
$\mathbf{w}$ for which $\sum_j c_j\mathbf{v}_j = \mathbf{w}$ is solvable. Sweeping the $k$
coefficients gives at most a $k$-dimensional family of outcomes — *at most*, because two
different coefficient choices can produce the *same* vector whenever the $\mathbf{v}_j$ are
related. In Question B's setup, for instance, the two vectors were unrelated and the span
was genuinely 2-dimensional; but recall the right panel of @fig-spans, where
$\mathbf{v}_2 = 1.8\,\mathbf{v}_1$ and *every* combination collapsed onto a single line.

The clean statement, which we will prove next article, is: the dimension of
$\operatorname{span}\{\mathbf{v}_1,\dots,\mathbf{v}_k\}$ equals the **number of the vectors
that are not linear combinations of the others** — the count of genuinely independent
directions among them. Equivalently it is the *rank* of the matrix whose columns are the
$\mathbf{v}_j$ ([GEOM-12]). For now, two consequences are all we need and they follow
directly from @def-span without the full theory:

- **Adding a vector already in the span never grows it.** If $\mathbf{v}_{k+1} \in
  \operatorname{span}\{\mathbf{v}_1,\dots,\mathbf{v}_k\}$, then
  $\operatorname{span}\{\mathbf{v}_1,\dots,\mathbf{v}_k,\mathbf{v}_{k+1}\} =
  \operatorname{span}\{\mathbf{v}_1,\dots,\mathbf{v}_k\}$. *Proof.* Write $S =
  \operatorname{span}\{\mathbf{v}_1,\dots,\mathbf{v}_k\}$ (the smaller set) and $T =
  \operatorname{span}\{\mathbf{v}_1,\dots,\mathbf{v}_k,\mathbf{v}_{k+1}\}$ (the larger). We
  show $T \subseteq S$ and $S \subseteq T$, which forces $T = S$.

  *$S \subseteq T$ (the easy inclusion, done explicitly so nothing is waved through).*
  Any $\mathbf{x} \in S$ is $\mathbf{x} = \sum_{j=1}^k a_j\mathbf{v}_j$ for some reals
  $a_j$. That very same expression is a combination of the longer list
  $\mathbf{v}_1,\dots,\mathbf{v}_{k+1}$ in which the coefficient on $\mathbf{v}_{k+1}$ is
  taken to be $0$: $\mathbf{x} = \sum_{j=1}^k a_j\mathbf{v}_j + 0\cdot\mathbf{v}_{k+1}$.
  Hence $\mathbf{x} \in T$. (This inclusion holds for *any* extra vector, related or not —
  appending a vector can only ever enlarge or preserve a span, never shrink it.)

  *$T \subseteq S$ (the inclusion the hypothesis buys us).* By assumption $\mathbf{v}_{k+1}
  \in S$, so there are reals $d_1,\dots,d_k$ with $\mathbf{v}_{k+1} = \sum_{j=1}^k
  d_j\mathbf{v}_j$. Take any $\mathbf{y} \in T$, say $\mathbf{y} = \sum_{j=1}^k
  b_j\mathbf{v}_j + b_{k+1}\mathbf{v}_{k+1}$. Substitute the expression for
  $\mathbf{v}_{k+1}$ and collect terms in each $\mathbf{v}_j$:

  $$
  \mathbf{y}
  = \sum_{j=1}^k b_j\mathbf{v}_j + b_{k+1}\sum_{j=1}^k d_j\mathbf{v}_j
  = \sum_{j=1}^k \bigl(b_j + b_{k+1}d_j\bigr)\mathbf{v}_j .
  $$

  Each coefficient $b_j + b_{k+1}d_j$ is a real number, so $\mathbf{y}$ is a combination of
  $\mathbf{v}_1,\dots,\mathbf{v}_k$ alone — that is, $\mathbf{y} \in S$. This "fold the
  coefficients in" step is exactly the closure of $S$ under combination that we proved in
  @thm-span-is-subspace. Both inclusions hold, so $T = S$. $\blacksquare$
- **The span of $k$ vectors has dimension at most $k$, and at most $n$.** At most $k$
  because there are only $k$ dials; at most $n$ because the whole thing lives inside
  $\mathbb{R}^n$, which is $n$-dimensional.

The gap between "at most $k$" and "exactly $k$" is the entire subject of linear
independence, and it is where redundancy — the data scientist's multicollinearity — lives.
We turn to it now as the honest edge case the Core promised.

# The honest edge case: when extra vectors buy no reach {#sec-collinear}

This is the section that separates "knows the definition of span" from "knows what span
*does* in data." We confront, head-on, the situation where you hold several vectors,
naively expect them to span a large region, and discover they span a much smaller one
because they are secretly related. In data this is **collinearity** (two features) and
**multicollinearity** (several), and it is one of the most common reasons a perfectly valid
regression returns nonsense coefficients.

## Two vectors that look independent but are not {#sec-look-independent}

Consider two feature columns from a hypothetical dataset of rental apartments: $x_1 =$
*floor area in square meters*, and $x_2 =$ *floor area in square feet*, measured on the
same units of length but reported in two systems. As vectors over the $n$ apartments, these
are not just *similar* — they are **exact scalar multiples**, because one square meter is
$10.7639$ square feet, so $\mathbf{x}_2 = 10.7639\,\mathbf{x}_1$ entrywise. Whatever
$\mathbf{x}_2$ contributes, $\mathbf{x}_1$ already contributed (scaled). Their span is a
**line**, not a plane, even though there are two of them and they "look" like two distinct
columns in the spreadsheet.

A subtler and more realistic version: $x_1 =$ *number of bedrooms*, $x_2 =$ *number of
bathrooms*, $x_3 =$ *total rooms*. If the dataset records *total rooms = bedrooms +
bathrooms + 1* (say, plus a kitchen) for every apartment, then $\mathbf{x}_3 = \mathbf{x}_1
+ \mathbf{x}_2 + \mathbf{1}$, where $\mathbf{1}$ is the all-ones vector (the intercept
column a regression silently adds). The third column is a *linear combination of columns
already present*. By the first bullet of @sec-howbig, it adds **zero** reach: the span of
all of them is the span of $\{\mathbf{1}, \mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2\}$, a 3-dimensional
subspace, even though there are four columns. One full degree of "apparent information" is
an illusion.

```{python}
#| label: fig-collinear
#| fig-cap: "Collinearity collapses a span. **Left:** two feature vectors that point in nearly the same direction (cosine of the angle between them $\\approx 0.9996$, printed below) — like square meters vs. square feet with a little measurement noise. Their span is *almost* a line: the shaded reachable region is a razor-thin sliver, not a fat plane, so to reach an off-sliver target you need enormous, near-cancelling coefficients. **Right:** the same two directions pulled apart to be genuinely distinct; now the span is a healthy, wide plane and every direction is cheaply reachable. The width of the sliver is what the *condition number* (GEOM-26) measures."
rng = np.random.default_rng(3)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.0, 3.8))

# --- Left: near-collinear pair ---
ax = axes[0]
u1 = np.array([1.5, 1.0]); u1 = u1 / np.linalg.norm(u1) * 2.2
u2 = u1 + np.array([0.06, -0.04])   # almost the same direction
# sample many combinations to visualize the (near-)reachable sliver with modest coeffs
cc = rng.uniform(-1.2, 1.2, size=(4000, 2))
pts = cc @ np.vstack([u1, u2])
ax.scatter(pts[:, 0], pts[:, 1], s=2, color=SPAN, alpha=0.10)
draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), u1, color=VEC, label=r"$\mathbf{x}_1$")
draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), u2, color=PALE, label=r"$\mathbf{x}_2$")
ax.set_title("near-collinear: a thin sliver")
ax.set_xlim(-4, 4); ax.set_ylim(-4, 4)

# --- Right: genuinely distinct pair ---
ax = axes[1]
w1 = np.array([2.2, 0.3]); w2 = np.array([0.3, 2.2])
cc = rng.uniform(-1.2, 1.2, size=(4000, 2))
pts = cc @ np.vstack([w1, w2])
ax.scatter(pts[:, 0], pts[:, 1], s=2, color=SPAN, alpha=0.10)
draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), w1, color=VEC, label=r"$\mathbf{x}_1$")
draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), w2, color=VEC, label=r"$\mathbf{x}_2$")
ax.set_title("distinct: a wide plane")
ax.set_xlim(-4, 4); ax.set_ylim(-4, 4)

for ax in axes:
    ax.axhline(0, color=GREY, lw=0.8); ax.axvline(0, color=GREY, lw=0.8)
    ax.set_aspect("equal"); ax.legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()

# Report how nearly parallel the left-panel pair is, as a concrete number. The cosine of
# the angle between two vectors is their dot product over the product of their lengths
# (this is GEOM-04's job to derive; here we only use it to put a number on "near-collinear").
# A cosine near 1 means the arrows point almost the same way -> the span is a thin sliver.
cos_angle = u1 @ u2 / (np.linalg.norm(u1) * np.linalg.norm(u2))
print(f"left panel: cos-angle between x1 and x2 = {cos_angle:.4f} (1.0 would be exactly collinear)")
```

## Why "almost collinear" is the dangerous case {#sec-almost}

Exact collinearity ($\mathbf{x}_2$ an *exact* multiple of $\mathbf{x}_1$) is the clean,
honest case: the span is exactly a line, the redundancy is visible, and most software will
refuse to fit or will drop a column. The treacherous case is **near-collinearity**, the
left panel of @fig-collinear, where the two vectors are *almost* parallel — distinct enough
that the span is technically the full plane, but so nearly aligned that the plane is a
razor-thin wedge in the directions that matter.

Here is the geometric mechanism, stated without yet having the dot product (that is
[GEOM-04]). To reach a target that lies slightly *off* the near-parallel direction, you must
take a large positive multiple of one vector and a large *negative* multiple of the other,
so that the bulk cancels and only the small perpendicular sliver survives. The coefficients
$c_1, c_2$ become enormous and nearly opposite. In a data setting those coefficients are
your regression weights: enormous, opposite, and exquisitely sensitive — nudge one data
point and they swing wildly, because a tiny change in the target requires a huge change in
the cancelling combination. The span did not *quite* collapse, but it came close enough
that solving inside it is numerically miserable.

This is the geometric seed of three things the track and the series develop in full, and we
name them so the reader sees the throughline:

- **Multicollinearity in regression** ([REGR-07]): the design matrix's columns nearly span a
  lower-dimensional subspace, so $X^\top X$ is nearly singular and the coefficient estimates
  have huge variance.
- **The condition number** ([GEOM-26]): the precise number measuring how thin the wedge is —
  the ratio of the longest to the shortest axis of the reachable region — and hence how much
  a small input error is amplified.
- **Rank deficiency and the four subspaces** ([GEOM-12]): exact collinearity is exact
  rank deficiency; the span's true dimension is the rank, and the gap between the column
  count and the rank is the redundancy.

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**More columns is not more information.** It is tempting to throw every available feature
into a model, reasoning that extra columns can only help. But if a new column lies in (or
near) the span of the columns you already have, it adds no reach and no genuine information
— it only *inflates the apparent dimension while the true dimension stands still*. The
model then has more coefficients than it has independent directions to pin them down, and
the surplus coefficients are not estimable separately: infinitely many coefficient vectors
produce the identical fit (exact case) or near-identical fits (near case). The cure is to
recognize the redundancy — drop a column, combine columns, or regularize — *before*
trusting any individual coefficient. Recognizing it **is** recognizing that a span did not
grow.
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"If I add a feature to my linear model and the fit doesn't improve at all, what could be
going on geometrically?"* The new feature vector is (exactly or nearly) already in the span
of the existing feature columns. Since a linear model's predictions are confined to that
span (the column space), a column that adds no new direction adds no new reachable
predictions, so the best achievable fit is unchanged. If the fit *appears* to improve only
trivially while the new coefficient is huge and unstable, you are in the near-collinear
regime: the column is *almost* redundant, and you are paying for a sliver of new reach with
a wildly sensitive coefficient. Either way the lesson is the same — examine whether your
columns span what you think they span.
:::

# A second worked example: spanning the whole space, and failing to {#sec-examples2}

We close the examples with a contrasting pair that pins down "when does a set of vectors
reach *everything*?" — the most practically loaded version of the span question, since
"the columns span all of $\mathbb{R}^n$" is what lets a linear system always have a
solution.

**Reaching everything in $\mathbb{R}^2$.** The two vectors $\mathbf{e}_1 = (1,0)^\top$ and
$\mathbf{e}_2 = (0,1)^\top$ — the standard axes — span all of $\mathbb{R}^2$: given any
target $(a,b)^\top$, the combination $a\mathbf{e}_1 + b\mathbf{e}_2 = a(1,0)^\top +
b(0,1)^\top = (a,0)^\top + (0,b)^\top = (a,b)^\top$ reproduces it exactly, with the
coefficients $(a,b)$ simply read off the target's own entries. But the axes are not
special: *any*
two vectors pointing in different directions do it. Take $\mathbf{u}_1 = (2,1)^\top$ and
$\mathbf{u}_2 = (1,3)^\top$. To reach an arbitrary $(a,b)^\top$ we solve $c_1\mathbf{u}_1 +
c_2\mathbf{u}_2 = (a,b)^\top$, i.e. the two equations $2c_1 + c_2 = a$ and $c_1 + 3c_2 = b$.
From the first, $c_2 = a - 2c_1$; substitute into the second: $c_1 + 3(a - 2c_1) = b$, so
$c_1 - 6c_1 + 3a = b$, giving $-5c_1 = b - 3a$ and $c_1 = (3a - b)/5$. Then $c_2 = a -
2(3a-b)/5 = (5a - 6a + 2b)/5 = (2b - a)/5$. A solution exists for *every* $(a,b)$ — the
span is all of $\mathbb{R}^2$.

**Failing to reach everything — the edge.** Now the honest near-failure. Take
$\mathbf{u}_1 = (2,1)^\top$ and $\mathbf{u}_2 = (4,2)^\top = 2\mathbf{u}_1$. The same setup
gives $2c_1 + 4c_2 = a$ and $c_1 + 2c_2 = b$. The first equation is exactly twice the
second on the left ($2c_1 + 4c_2 = 2(c_1 + 2c_2)$), so it demands $a = 2b$. If $a \neq 2b$,
*no* solution exists: the target is unreachable. The span is only the line $\{(2t, t)\}$,
and any target off that line is forever out of reach. @fig-reach contrasts the two.

```{python}
#| label: fig-reach
#| fig-cap: "When do two vectors reach all of $\\mathbb{R}^2$? **Left:** $\\mathbf{u}_1=(2,1)$ and $\\mathbf{u}_2=(1,3)$ point in different directions and span the whole plane — an arbitrary target (green star) is reachable, here $(3,2)=c_1\\mathbf{u}_1+c_2\\mathbf{u}_2$ with the coefficients solved in the text. **Right:** $\\mathbf{u}_2=(4,2)=2\\mathbf{u}_1$ is collinear with $\\mathbf{u}_1$; the span is just the line $a=2b$, and the same target $(3,2)$ now floats off it (red star) and is unreachable. Two vectors span the plane exactly when they are not collinear."
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.0, 3.9))
target = np.array([3.0, 2.0])

# --- Left: spanning the plane ---
ax = axes[0]
u1 = np.array([2.0, 1.0]); u2 = np.array([1.0, 3.0])
ax.fill([-5, 5, 5, -5], [-5, -5, 5, 5], color=SPAN, alpha=0.08)
draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), u1, color=VEC, label=r"$\mathbf{u}_1$")
draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), u2, color=VEC, label=r"$\mathbf{u}_2$")
ax.scatter(*target, color=ACCENT, marker="*", s=260, zorder=6, edgecolor="white")
ax.text(target[0], target[1], r"  $(3,2)$ reachable", color=ACCENT, fontsize=10)
ax.set_title("two directions span the plane")
ax.set_xlim(-1, 5); ax.set_ylim(-1, 5)

# --- Right: collinear, spans only a line ---
ax = axes[1]
u1 = np.array([2.0, 1.0]); u2 = np.array([4.0, 2.0])
ts = np.linspace(-2, 2.5, 50)
ln = np.outer(ts, u1)
ax.plot(ln[:, 0], ln[:, 1], color=SPAN, lw=2.2, label=r"span (line $a=2b$)")
draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), u2, color=PALE, label=r"$\mathbf{u}_2=2\mathbf{u}_1$")
draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), u1, color=VEC, label=r"$\mathbf{u}_1$")
ax.scatter(*target, color=SPAN, marker="*", s=260, zorder=6, edgecolor="white")
ax.text(target[0], target[1], r"  $(3,2)$ unreachable", color=SPAN, fontsize=10)
ax.set_title("collinear: span is only a line")
ax.set_xlim(-1, 5); ax.set_ylim(-1, 5)

for ax in axes:
    ax.axhline(0, color=GREY, lw=0.8); ax.axvline(0, color=GREY, lw=0.8)
    ax.set_aspect("equal"); ax.legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

The contrast is the whole lesson of the article in one figure: the same *count* of vectors
(two) produces either the full plane or a mere line, decided entirely by whether the second
vector pointed somewhere genuinely new. Reaching "everything" in $\mathbb{R}^n$ requires the
span to fill all $n$ dimensions, which requires $n$ vectors *that each add a new direction*
— the precise condition (a **basis**) that [GEOM-03] is built to deliver.

# Failure modes and the edge of applicability {#sec-failure}

The concepts of span and subspace are exact — there is no approximation to break, the way a
regression line can be wrong. The failure modes here are failures of *recognition* and of
*notation*, plus the numerical edge where exactness meets floating point. They are worth
collecting.

## The affine trap: forgetting the origin {#sec-affine-trap}

The single most common error is treating an **affine** set (a flat set that misses the
origin) as a subspace. We met it in the Pitfall of @sec-subspace-def: the line $y = x+1$,
or a data cloud that has not been centered. The diagnostic is mechanical — *does it contain
$\mathbf{0}$, and is it closed?* — and the fix is mechanical too: **center the data**
(subtract the mean from each column) to slide the affine set to the origin, where it
becomes a genuine subspace. This is exactly why the data tracks center before computing
covariance, before PCA, and before any projection: the geometry of subspaces only applies
once the origin is where it belongs. When a method *does* care about the offset (an
intercept term in regression), the standard move is to append the all-ones column
$\mathbf{1}$ and span the affine family as a subspace in one-higher dimension — turning an
affine question into a span question.

## The degenerate span: all-zero or duplicate vectors {#sec-degenerate}

If you hand the span the zero vector, it contributes nothing:
$\operatorname{span}\{\mathbf{v}_1,\dots,\mathbf{v}_k,\mathbf{0}\} =
\operatorname{span}\{\mathbf{v}_1,\dots,\mathbf{v}_k\}$, because any coefficient on
$\mathbf{0}$ produces $\mathbf{0}$ and changes no combination. Likewise a duplicated vector,
or any exact linear combination of the others, adds no reach (the first bullet of
@sec-howbig). These are the exact-collinearity cases. They are not errors — the span is
perfectly well-defined — but they are the situations where "I have $k$ vectors" badly
overstates "my span is $k$-dimensional." The honest dimension is the count of
non-redundant directions, the rank, which [GEOM-03] and [GEOM-12] compute.

## The numerical edge: when "in the span" becomes "almost" {#sec-numerical}

On a computer, the crisp distinction between "in the span" and "not in the span" blurs.
Floating-point arithmetic cannot represent most reals exactly, so a target that is
*mathematically* in the span may, after rounding, fail the consistency check by a hair, and
a near-collinear pair (left panel of @fig-collinear) may be reported as exactly collinear or
as barely independent depending on the last bit. This is not a flaw in the concept; it is
the reason the *condition number* exists. The practical rule the track will justify in
[GEOM-26]: do not test "is $\mathbf{w}$ in the span?" by checking whether a residual is
*exactly* zero — that is fragile — and do not test rank deficiency by checking whether a
determinant is exactly zero. Use a tolerance, and prefer the SVD's singular values
([GEOM-20]), which report *how close* to rank-deficient you are on a meaningful scale,
rather than a yes/no that floating point cannot honestly deliver.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"When is the span/subspace framework the right lens, and when is it not?"* Use it whenever
your problem is genuinely about **linear** reach — what a linear model can predict, which
systems $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$ are solvable, what directions a set of features covers,
what a projection projects onto. It is exactly the right tool there and underlies the entire
linear-algebra half of data science. It is the **wrong** lens when the relationships are
nonlinear: the set of points a *neural network* or a *kernel method* can reach is a curved
manifold, not a flat subspace, and forcing it into span language loses the curvature that
matters (the kernel trick, [CLAS], deliberately maps into a high-dimensional space precisely
to make a nonlinear reachable set *look* linear there). And it is the wrong lens for sets
that are flat but off-origin until you center them (the affine trap). The discipline:
confirm the operations in play really are "add and scale," and that the origin is where you
think it is.
:::

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
You arrived from **[GEOM-01 *Adding and Scaling*]**, which gave you the linear combination as
the one move linear algebra permits. This article turned that single move loose — letting
its coefficients range over all of $\mathbb{R}$ — and named the reachable set (**span**) and
its shape (**subspace**), proving they are one object. From here the build continues:

- **[GEOM-03 *Linear Independence, Basis, and Dimension*]** — the immediate sequel. We
  asked "how big is the span?" and could only answer "at most $k$." GEOM-03 answers it
  exactly: a span's dimension is the number of *independent* directions in it, a basis is a
  minimal spanning set, and the dimension bound we borrowed in @thm-subspace-is-span is
  proved there in full.
- **[GEOM-06 *Orthogonal Projection onto a Subspace*]** — the keystone. Once we know a span
  is a subspace, the central operation of the whole series is *projecting a point onto it* —
  finding the closest reachable point to an unreachable target, exactly the situation of
  $\mathbf{w}'$ in @sec-membership. That is least squares.
- **[GEOM-08 *Matrices as Linear Maps*]** and **[GEOM-11 *Solving $Ax=b$*]** — make the
  membership test fully algebraic: $A\mathbf{c}=\mathbf{w}$ is solvable exactly when
  $\mathbf{w}$ is in the span of $A$'s columns (the **column space**).
- **[GEOM-12 *Rank and the Four Fundamental Subspaces*]** — the column space is a span and a
  subspace; its dimension is the rank; and the collinearity collapse of @sec-collinear
  becomes rank deficiency.
- **Across to [REGR-07 *Multicollinearity*]** — the data-science payoff: when feature columns
  nearly fail to add reach, coefficients become unstable. That article links straight back
  here for the geometry.
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **Primary sources.** Hermann Grassmann's *Die lineale Ausdehnungslehre* (1844, expanded
  1862) is the origin of treating "all combinations of given elements, closed under the
  operations" as a structure in its own right — span and subspace in embryo. Giuseppe
  Peano's *Calcolo geometrico* (1888) gave the modern axioms of a vector space, from which
  the subspace conditions of @def-subspace fall out directly.
- **The recommended modern treatments.** Gilbert Strang's *Introduction to Linear Algebra*
  develops the column space and its span as the organizing idea of the subject (the "four
  subspaces" framing this track adopts in [GEOM-12]); Sheldon Axler's *Linear Algebra Done
  Right* gives the cleanest axiomatic account of subspaces and spanning, at a slightly more
  abstract pitch than we need here.
- **Open threads this article deliberately deferred.** *Exactly how big is a span, and is
  the count well-defined?* — dimension and its invariance, [GEOM-03]. *What is the closest
  reachable point to an unreachable target?* — orthogonal projection, [GEOM-06], which needs
  the dot product of [GEOM-04] first. *How do I read the dimension of a span off a matrix
  reliably, even numerically?* — rank ([GEOM-12]) and the singular values ([GEOM-20]). Each
  is a later chapter of the same story you started here: *what can I reach, and how much room
  is there to reach it in?*
